query: metabolic fate of intermediate density lipoprotein in human plasma

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The ApoE isoform ApoE2 is clinically significant due to its reduced affinity for LRP and LDLR. Poor binding of this isoform decreases hepatic clearance of IDL particles and elevates serum triglyceride levels. [6] ApoCIII is considered pathogenic, as it promotes atherosclerosis by delaying the clearance of ApoB-containing lipoproteins and prolonging their presence in plasma. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Upon liberation in plasma, VLDL is degraded to IDL through the hydrolytic action of LPL on the capillary endothelium. LPL acts on VLDL triglycerides via its N-terminal domain, releasing free fatty acids, which are subsequently taken up by adipocytes or myocytes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP), a plasma glycoprotein synthesized in the liver, facilitates the transfer of cholesteryl esters from HDL and LDL particles to VLDL particles in exchange for triglycerides. These VLDL particles are subsequently internalized by hepatocytes, providing an alternative pathway for cholesterol delivery to the liver. CETP levels are elevated in patients with metabolic syndrome. [14] The interaction between HDL, CETP, and LDL particles is believed to generate small, dense LDL particles, which contribute to atherosclerosis in patients with metabolic syndrome. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Lipase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, LPL plays a significant role in the progression of atherosclerosis. LPL is a component in atherosclerotic lesions, which derive from macrophages. Furthermore, patients with advanced atherosclerosis were found to have elevated LPL mass and activity in their post-heparin plasma. LPL contributes to atherogenic lipoprotein formation. LPL acts on chylomicrons and VLDL to hydrolyze triglycerides from them and form VLDL remnants and chylomicron remnants. VLDL remnants and chylomicron remnants are composed of a high concentration of cholesterol esters and then get incorporated into macrophages. LPL creates free fatty acids, and macrophages then re-esterify them, accumulating cholesterol esters in macrophages, causing macrophages to transform into foam cells. LPL catalyzed the conversion of VLDL to Intermediate density lipoproteins (IDL), which hepatic lipases then convert to low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Foam cells also form when macrophages incorporate oxidized LDL via their scavenger receptor in the vascular endothelium. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction between water-fearing and fat-loving displays more clearly during lipid transport in plasma. Both cholesterol and triglycerides are nonpolar lipid molecules. Therefore, they must travel in the polar plasma with the help of lipoprotein particles. The main goal of lipoprotein is to help transport lipids (hydrophobic) in water. The structure of lipoprotein consists of triglycerides, cholesterol, phospholipids, and apolipoproteins. Apolipoproteins mainly function as carrier proteins but also serve as cofactors for enzymes that metabolize lipoproteins and help in lipid component exchange among lipoproteins. Some examples of lipoproteins include chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). Each one is used in a different phase of lipid transport. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: ApoCII is essential to LPL activation. During the lipolytic process, ApoCII is transferred to HDL particles, leading to self-arrest of LPL activity. Approximately half of the newly formed IDL particles are taken up by hepatocytes through LDLR-ApoE-mediated endocytosis. Hepatic lipase, localized on the sinusoidal surface, functions independently of ApoCII and continues hydrolyzing triglycerides and phospholipids in the remaining IDL particles, ultimately leading to LDL formation. The fate of LDL particles largely depends on the number of LDLRs on the hepatocyte surface, as a reduced number of LDLRs is associated with decreased clearance and elevated LDL levels. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond its direct relation with atherosclerosis, VLDL also plays an important role in metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance. In a normal physiologic state, insulin suppresses the production and secretion of VLDL. In subjects with insulin resistance, an enhanced synthesis and reduced clearance of VLDL is often detected as increased plasma triglyceride levels. [31] [32] In these subjects with insulin resistance, there is also a dysfunction in lipid storage in adipose tissue, making them prone to hyperlipidemia. Further, VLDL in metabolic syndrome can induce macrophage apoptosis by increasing reactive oxygen species production. These cytotoxic properties cause VLDL to be highly pro-inflammatory and atherogenic. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: IDL particles are formed through the LPL-mediated removal of triglycerides from VLDL. Hepatic lipase subsequently converts these particles to LDL. Thus, IDL represents an essential transition state and determines the cholesterol levels of circulating LDL particles. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: IDL particles exhibit a reduced FCR and prolonged plasma residence in patients with metabolic syndrome. [39] Such tendencies may explain the association between IDL cholesterol levels and the progression of metabolic syndrome. [40] Lipid-lowering drugs, such as fibrates and statins, can enhance the FCR of IDL particles, lowering both IDL and LDL cholesterol levels. Therefore, these agents may be used to manage dyslipidemia in metabolic syndrome. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly a lipophilic molecule, it does not dissolve well in the blood. For this reason, it is packaged in lipoproteins that have phospholipid and apolipoprotein. [3] Lipoproteins are made up of a lipid core (which can contain cholesterol esters and triglycerides) and a hydrophilic outer membrane comprising phospholipid, apolipoprotein, and free cholesterol. This allows the lipid molecules to move around the body through the blood and be transported to cells that need them. There are several types of lipoproteins that travel through the blood, and they each have different purposes. There are high-density lipoproteins (HDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL). Notably, LDL particles are thought to act as a major transporter of cholesterol; at least two-thirds of circulating cholesterol resides in LDL to the peripheral tissues. Conversely, HDL molecules are thought to do the opposite. They take excess cholesterol and return it to the liver for excretion. Clinically, these two lipoproteins are significant since high LDL and low HDL increase patients' risk of atherosclerotic vascular diseases. [4] [5] See Figure. The Molecular Structure of Cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: IDL levels fluctuate due to various extrinsic and intrinsic factors. An increase may result from either accelerated production or impaired clearance of metabolites. Influx is influenced by the production and fractional catabolic rate (FCR) of VLDL particles, while efflux depends on hepatic lipase activity and hepatic clearance mediated by ApoE.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Incomplete lipidation results in ubiquitinylation and degradation of precursor particles, as seen in abetalipoproteinemia, a condition characterized by the loss of functional MTP, reducing the assembly of VLDL and chylomicrons. [10] In contrast, increased production and stability of these precursor particles occur in insulin resistance, which is associated with diabetes. Consequently, large VLDL particles are released, contributing to hypertriglyceridemia in patients with diabetes. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids are transported between cells through 5 types of lipoproteins, listed in order of increasing density: chylomicrons, very low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). IDL particles are VLDL remnants formed after the partial removal of triglycerides in muscle and adipose tissue. These biomolecules represent an intermediate stage between LDL and VLDL in the catabolic cascade.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoprotein metabolism exhibits heterogeneity within each lipoprotein fraction, with multiple parallel biochemical pathways contributing to the formation of distinct lipoprotein subfractions, each with unique metabolic properties. For instance, VLDL consists of 2 subfractions, VLDL1 and VLDL2, that may be separated by ultracentrifugation. VLDL1, or large VLDL particles, primarily transport triglycerides and are implicated in insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. [16] In contrast, VLDL2, or small VLDL particles, are cholesterol-rich and contribute to variations in LDL cholesterol levels. Elevated VLDL2 levels are observed in familial hypercholesterolemia. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The Apo C-II in mature VLDL activates LPL in the capillary endothelium of tissues to cleave their stored triglycerides into 1 monoglyceride and 2 free fatty acids, releasing them from the VLDL and allowing them to be delivered to the tissues to be used for storage or energy production. [9] [10] Insulin also upregulates Apo C-II's activity and increases triglyceride hydrolysis into free fatty acids for tissue absorption. Simultaneously, the cholesterol ester transfer protein (CETP) promotes the exchange of VLDL’s triglycerides and phospholipids for HDL’s cholesterol esters. After releasing enough triglycerides either into the tissues or to HDL, the core of the VLDL gets reduced, and the VLDL remnant is now called IDL. Approximately half of the circulating IDL is then taken by the liver by binding to the Apo B-100 and Apo-E. The remaining half of the IDL loses its Apo-E and transforms into LDL through the action of the hepatic lipase.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein A -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma concentrations of Lp(a) and the apo(a) isoform are inversely related. [4] The variation in isoforms is induced by the number of kringle IV repeats in the LP(a) gene. The variation in kringle units leads to the variable levels of Lp(a) observed in the general population. In general, individuals with fewer kringle repeats tend to have smaller Lp(a) particles but higher serum levels. In addition, larger isoforms of apo(a) lead to an increased accumulation of its precursor intracellularly within the endoplasmic reticulum. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis, which leads to reductions in low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and triglycerides and modest increases in high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are widely used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related benefits, and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin and fluvastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, which leads to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As members of the same pharmacologic group, atorvastatin and fluvastatin share similar indications for treating hypercholesterolemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, as well as comparable safety considerations such as the potential for myopathy and drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing both agents as part of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians apply class‑wide prescribing guidelines while selecting a specific statin based on potency, pharmacokinetic profile, and individual patient tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 497
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise several specific agents that are members of this drug class. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are all included within the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group, reflecting their shared mechanism of inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase to lower cholesterol levels. Recognizing these agents as members of the same therapeutic class aids clinicians in understanding their common pharmacologic effects and in selecting appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their inclusion in the plain lipid‑modifying drug category underscores their primary role in managing hyperlipidemia and preventing related cardiovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 414
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, a group classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs that are used to lower cholesterol levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1847
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class that includes the individual agents atorvastatin, fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. All four medications share the same mechanism of action— inhibition of the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis. By blocking this enzyme, these agents lower low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and total cholesterol levels, thereby reducing the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and understand class‑related effects, dosing considerations, and potential adverse reactions such as myopathy or liver enzyme elevations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.
